title: CHiP-RLCP Fallback Note: Hierarchical Diagonal-GMM Posteriors for Localized Conformal Prediction
description: This workspace does not implement the proposed LearnSPN-style probabilistic circuit. The study is therefore reframed as a scoped negative-result note that evaluates a two-level hierarchical diagonal-GMM fallback inside the same local conformal pipeline, with explicit comparison against split CP, class-conditional CP, kNN localization, flat GMM localization, and a finite-group batch multivalid thresholding baseline.
motivation: The original PC claim cannot be defended without an actual SPN/PC implementation and exact internal posterior inference. The defensible question for this attempt is narrower: whether the already-implemented hierarchical GMM fallback offers any subgroup-coverage advantage over simpler flat or explicit-group baselines under the same shared score and CPU budget.
proposed_approach: Fit a hierarchical diagonal-GMM joint classifier p(x,y), use s(x,y)=1-p(y|x) as the common score, derive coarse and fine soft memberships from its posterior responsibilities, and evaluate localized weighted conformal prediction plus ablations on synthetic hierarchy, Anuran taxonomy, and Mice Protein groups. Report the study as a negative-result note if hierarchical localization is at parity with or worse than flat GMM and batch multivalid baselines.
hypothesis: The hierarchical GMM fallback may improve worst external-group coverage over Split CP and kNN localization on structured datasets, but it is likely to be statistically tied with or worse than flat GMM localization and explicit multivalid batch thresholding once the evaluation groups are fixed.
success_criteria: All reported numbers come from executed code with saved logs and seedwise artifacts.
At least 3 random seeds are run on synthetic, Anuran, and Mice, with mean, standard deviation, and confidence intervals.
The benchmark includes at least 2 meaningful baselines and all four planned ablations of the hierarchical fallback.
Per-example artifacts include group membership strings, and dataset/group summary artifacts include the missing intersection groups and preprocessing statistics.
The final note states honestly whether the hierarchical fallback is better, tied, or worse than flat GMM and finite-group multivalid baselines.
